replies.msg:
you can say transfer/ send/ transmit sth abroad, because transfer is a directional verb, i.e. transfer A (abroad) to B

this doesn't apply to "receive abroad, because receive isn't a directional verb
you should instead say receive sth from abroad
non-directional verb + abroad (e.g. live abroad, study abroad) means performing an action in a foreign country, so to receive money abroad = to receive money outside HK (not from outside HK)

in this case, the simplest way to put it would be: to receive money from the UK

比較唔常用

you can say transfer/ send/ transmit sth abroad, because transfer is a directional verb, i.e. transfer A (abroad) to B

this doesn't apply to "receive abroad, because receive isn't a directional verb
you should instead say receive sth from abroad
non-directional verb + abroad (e.g. live abroad, study abroad) means performing an action in a foreign country, so to receive money abroad = to receive money outside HK (not from outside HK)

in this case, the simplest way to put it would be: to receive money from the UK

比較唔常用

you can say transfer/ send/ transmit sth abroad, because transfer is a directional verb, i.e. transfer A (abroad) to B

this doesn't apply to "receive abroad, because receive isn't a directional verb
you should instead say receive sth from abroad
non-directional verb + abroad (e.g. live abroad, study abroad) means performing an action in a foreign country, so to receive money abroad = to receive money outside HK (not from outside HK)

in this case, the simplest way to put it would be: to receive money from the UK
